1: 'This is what the Lord GOD showed me: behold, a basket of summer fruit.' The fourth vision uses a wordplay that requires knowing Hebrew. A basket of qayitz (summer fruit — figs, grapes, pomegranates at their ripest). The pun in v2: YHWH says 'the qetz (end) has come upon my people Israel.' The summer fruit is ripe — fully mature, at its peak — and so is Israel's judgment: ready for harvest. The summer fruit (qayitz) was gathered at the end of the agricultural year; the qetz (end) is the end of Israel's national existence. The vision is an acted parable of completed ripeness.
2: 'He said, Amos, what do you see? And I said, A basket of summer fruit. Then the LORD said to me, The end has come upon my people Israel; I will never again pass by them.' The wordplay qayitz/qetz establishes that the summer harvest is a sign of the approaching end. 'I will never again pass by them' repeats Amos 7:8 — definitively: the period of intercession and relenting is over. The phrase recurs a second time (from Amos 7:8) to emphasize the finality. In 7:8 it followed the plumb line vision; here it follows the summer fruit, confirming the trajectory of the last two visions toward irreversible judgment.
3: 'The songs of the temple shall become wailings in that day, declares the Lord GOD. So many dead bodies! They are thrown everywhere. Silence!' 'Songs of the temple' (shirot heikal) — the sacred songs of the Bethel sanctuary (which had professional musicians and elaborate worship, cf. Amos 5:23) become funeral wailings. 'In every place they are thrown' (bekhol-maqom hishlich) — the random casting of unburied bodies everywhere, the sign of total defeat and the activation of the covenant curse (Deuteronomy 28:25-26: 'your dead bodies shall be food for all birds of the air'). 'Silence!' (has) — the command to silence in the face of overwhelming death.
4: 'Hear this, you who trample on the needy and bring the poor of the land to an end.' The economic oracle that follows the vision — the specific social crime that makes Israel ripe for judgment. 'Trample on the needy' (hashoa'afim ebyon) and 'bring the poor to an end' (lashbit aniyei-aretz) — both verbs are strong: trampling/panting after the poor, and actively eliminating the poor class (by absorbing their land, driving them into debt slavery). This directly indicts the landholding elite of Jeroboam II's prosperous kingdom.
5: 'Saying, When will the new moon be over, that we may sell grain? And the Sabbath, that we may offer wheat for sale, that we may make the ephah small and the shekel great and deal deceitfully with false balances.' The religious calendar — new moon (rosh chodesh) and Sabbath (shabbat) — were both days of commercial rest (cf. 2 Kings 4:23: 'Why will you go to him today? It is neither new moon nor sabbath'). The merchants observe the form (they cease trading on holy days) while their hearts are on the resumption of fraud. The double fraud: a small ephah (short measure for the buyer) and a large shekel (overweight payment demand from the seller) simultaneously extracted maximum profit from every transaction.
6: 'That we may buy the poor for silver and the needy for a pair of sandals and sell the trash of the wheat?' Three practices condemned: (1) buying the poor as slaves for debt (silver), (2) buying the needy for a pair of sandals (either a pun on legal custom — sandal exchange in property transfer, Ruth 4:7 — or meaning they will enslave someone for a trivial debt), (3) selling the refuse of the wheat (maphel bar) — the chaff, husks, and dirt swept up from the threshing floor, mixed with grain and sold as if it were pure wheat. The three practices together represent systematic fraud targeting the vulnerable.
7: 'The LORD has sworn by the pride of Jacob: Surely I will never forget any of their deeds.' 'The pride of Jacob' (ge'on Ya'aqov) — used in 6:8 for what YHWH abhors in Israel's self-regard; here it is a divine oath title (YHWH's own pride/majesty). The paradox is that the same term applies both to the condemned pride of Israel's elite and to the divine glory that is the basis of the oath. 'I will never forget any of their deeds' — the divine memory as guarantee of judgment. In Deuteronomy 32:34 YHWH says their punishment is 'sealed up in my treasuries'; here the explicit promise is that economic oppression will not be forgotten.
8: 'Shall not the land tremble on this account, and everyone mourn who dwells in it, and all of it rise like the Nile, and be tossed about and sink again, like the Nile of Egypt?' The land mourning (avela ha-aretz) — the covenant land responding physically to moral catastrophe, as in Hosea 4:3 and Joel 1:10. The Nile flood metaphor (ke'or Mitzrayim) — the Nile's seasonal flooding, which rises dramatically and then falls, is used as an image of land upheaval. The Nile's flood was both the source of Egyptian agricultural abundance and a powerful natural force; its application to the land of Israel suggests a catastrophic inundation of the covenant land.
9: 'And on that day, declares the Lord GOD, I will make the sun go down at noon and darken the earth in broad daylight.' 'The sun going down at noon' (vehevati hashemesh batzoharayim) — darkness at noon is the image of total disorientation; noon is the height of light, not the time for darkness. Whether this refers to a solar eclipse (a solar eclipse was visible in Assyria in 763 BCE and may have been seen in the Levant) or is purely metaphorical is debated. The image recurs in Joel 2:10, 3:15 and becomes the sign of the Day of YHWH. Mark 15:33 records darkness from noon to three at the crucifixion — an event the NT reads against this background.
10: 'I will turn your feasts into mourning and all your songs into lamentation; I will bring sackcloth on every waist and baldness on every head; I will make it like the mourning for an only son and the end of it like a bitter day.' The festival-to-mourning reversal: the three pilgrimage feasts (chagim) and their songs become funeral rites. 'Baldness on every head' (qorchah al-kol-rosh) was a mourning practice (Isaiah 22:12, Jeremiah 16:6, Micah 1:16) — shaving the head as a sign of grief. 'Mourning for an only son' (evel yachid) — the most extreme grief, the death of the sole heir, the end of the family line. Jeremiah 6:26 and Zechariah 12:10 use the same image.
11: 'Behold, the days are coming, declares the Lord GOD, when I will send a famine on the land — not a famine of bread, nor a thirst for water, but of hearing the words of the LORD.' The famine of the word (ra'av lishmo'a et divrei YHWH) — one of Amos's most distinctive and influential oracles. Having rejected the prophetic word, Israel will find itself in a situation where they desperately seek it and cannot find it. The word of YHWH, freely available and constantly refused, will be removed. The relationship between prophetic rejection and prophetic silence is explicit: 'they would not listen' (Amos 2:12, Amos 7:12-13) leads to 'they shall seek the word of the LORD and not find it.'
12: 'They shall wander from sea to sea, and from north to east; they shall run to and fro, to seek the word of the LORD, but they shall not find it.' The frantic, undirected search — 'from sea to sea' (miyam ad-yam) and 'from north to east' (rather than the expected north to south) suggesting disorientation. The Diaspora context: the northern Israelites scattered among the nations would indeed find themselves in places where access to prophetic revelation and covenant worship was impossible. The 'seeking and not finding' of the word echoes Proverbs 1:28 (the fool seeks wisdom after rejecting it, but wisdom turns away) — applied here nationally.
13: 'In that day the lovely virgins and the young men shall faint for thirst.' The most vigorous members of the population — 'lovely virgins' (betulot tovot) and young men (bahurim) — fainting from thirst. Both groups represent the prime of Israel's demographic future: if they collapse, the nation has no next generation. The thirst is presumably both literal (the drought that follows agricultural collapse) and metaphorical (the absence of the word in v11-12). The specific mention of these two groups echoes Joel 1:8 (the virgin lamenting), connecting the locust-plague imagery of Joel with Amos's judgment oracle.
14: 'Those who swear by the Guilt of Samaria, and say, As your god lives, O Dan, and, As the Way of Beersheba lives — they shall fall and never rise again.' 'The Guilt of Samaria' (ashmat Shomron) — possibly the golden calf of Bethel (near Samaria) called 'guilt/sin' (asham) as a polemical repointing; alternatively 'Ashima,' a deity of Hamath (2 Kings 17:30). 'As your god lives, O Dan' — the golden calf at Dan (1 Kings 12:29), invoked in oath-formulas. 'The Way of Beersheba' (derekh Be'er-Sheva) — the pilgrimage route to the ancient patriarchal site in the south. All three are dismissed together as false cult objects; those who swear by them will fall irreversibly.
